question_title: Will Chris Nanos still be Pima County Sheriff on May 31, 2026?
summary: Chris Nanos is currently serving as Pima County Sheriff and has been publicly active in his role through at least May 12, 2026. With only 9 days until the May 31 resolution date, every formal removal mechanism is either blocked or has already been attempted and failed: the Board of Supervisors voted not to remove him on May 12-13, a recall is procedurally impossible within the timeframe, the AG perjury referral was made only ~10 days ago (investigations take months), and the FBI probe shows no signs of imminent action. Nanos shows no indication of planning to resign — he continues giving press conferences and actively defending his position. The only realistic NO scenarios are a surprise voluntary resignation under pressure or an unexpected health/legal emergency, which collectively warrant about 4% probability. Strong YES at 96%.

factors.description: Status quo: Nanos is currently serving as sheriff and was publicly active as recently as May 12. Status quo resolves YES.
Board of Supervisors explicitly voted NOT to remove him on May 12-13, 2026. The primary political removal mechanism has been attempted and failed.
Recall is procedurally impossible before May 31 — requires 120 days of signature collection plus an election. This path is completely blocked.
AG perjury referral was just made ~10 days ago. Criminal investigations and prosecutions take months to years. No charges, no court action expected in 9 days.
FBI investigation ongoing but no public indication of imminent charges or action.
Nanos has shown no signs of resignation — continues giving media updates and acting in official capacity.
Only 9 days remain until resolution — extremely short window for any removal mechanism to succeed.

Residual risk: surprise resignation under mounting pressure (FBI probe, AG referral, no-confidence vote, massive media scrutiny), unexpected health event, or emergency court order. These are low-probability but real tail events.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 96% YES — Chris Nanos will still be Pima County Sheriff on May 31, 2026**

**Key reasoning:**
- **Status quo strongly favors YES**: Nanos is currently in office and has been publicly active through at least May 12, giving press conferences in his official capacity
- **Board removal failed**: The Board of Supervisors voted not to remove him on May 12-13 — the primary political removal path has been tried and exhausted
- **Recall is impossible**: Arizona recall requires 120 days of signature collection plus an election — structurally cannot complete in 9 days
- **Investigations are early-stage**: The AG perjury referral was made only ~10 days ago; the FBI probe has no imminent action signals. Criminal proceedings take months to years.
- **No resignation signals**: Nanos continues to actively work, give media updates, and defend his position
- **Only 9 days remain**: This extremely short window blocks virtually all formal removal mechanisms

**Residual 4% NO risk** accounts for: surprise voluntary resignation under extraordinary pressure (FBI, AG, no-confidence vote, national media scrutiny), unexpected health emergency, or an unforeseen rapid legal action.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Chris Nanos still be Pima County Sheriff on May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43568)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if Chris Nanos is the sitting Sheriff of Pima County, Arizona at 23:59 Mountain Standard Time on May 31, 2026 — meaning he has not resigned, been removed from office, been recalled, died, or otherwise ceased to hold the office of Sheriff by that time. The primary resolution source is the Pima County Sheriff's Department official website (https://www.pimasheriff.org/), which identifies the sitting sheriff; the question resolves NO if that site, the Pima County government's official communications, or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) report that Nanos no longer holds the office of Pima County Sheriff as of that time. A temporary delegation of duties to an undersheriff or acting official while Nanos remains the legal officeholder (e.g., for illness or travel) does not by itself resolve the question NO.
fine_print: If Nanos has been formally suspended from his duties as Sheriff (e.g., by court order or pursuant to Arizona statute) such that he is no longer exercising the office as of May 31, 2026, the question resolves NO even if he has not formally resigned or been removed. If the primary source is unavailable, resolution will rely on [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).

input.anchor: Base rate for an elected county sheriff remaining in office over any given 9-day period: ~99%+. Elected officials very rarely leave office in such short windows. Adjusted down slightly because Nanos faces active political pressure (Board referral, perjury allegations, FBI probe, no-confidence vote from deputies).
input.assessment:
This is a strong YES. The question asks whether Nanos will still be sheriff in 9 days. He is currently in office. Every formal removal mechanism has either been attempted and failed (Board vote), or is procedurally impossible within the timeframe (recall). The AG perjury referral was made only ~10 days ago and criminal investigations take months minimum. The FBI investigation has no public indication of being near completion.

The only realistic NO scenarios are: (1) Surprise voluntary resignation — unlikely given Nanos's behavior pattern of actively defending his position and continuing to work, (2) Emergency court-ordered suspension — would require someone filing a judicial removal petition AND a judge issuing an emergency suspension, with no evidence any such filing exists, (3) Death or severe incapacitation — base rate for any 9-day window is very low (~0.05% for a middle-aged man).

The political pressure is real but has already peaked and been weathered. The Board's decision not to remove him was the critical moment, and it passed without action. With 9 days remaining, inertia overwhelmingly favors YES.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is ~99% base rate for any sheriff staying in office over 9 days. Adjusting down to ~96% to account for the unusual political pressure. This still gives a ~4% chance of removal or resignation, which accounts for low-probability tail events like a surprise resignation under pressure or unexpected developments.
input.key_uncertainties: The main uncertainty is whether Nanos might voluntarily resign. He's under enormous public pressure and scrutiny. However, his observed behavior (continuing to give press conferences, fighting the removal attempt) strongly suggests he intends to stay.
input.tool_audit: Research tool provided solid background on Nanos's current status and Arizona removal procedures. Web search confirmed the May 12-13 Board vote and current state of affairs. No tool failures. All searches returned relevant results.
input.update_triggers: 1. News of Nanos filing resignation or announcing intent to resign. 2. AG announcing expedited perjury charges. 3. Federal indictment from FBI investigation. 4. Emergency judicial removal petition filed in Superior Court. 5. Health emergency.
input.process_reflection: This was a straightforward question requiring mostly factual research about current status and legal procedures. The research tool and web search worked well in parallel. The question is well-suited to the binary framework — clear resolution criteria, short time horizon, and identifiable mechanisms for change.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated evidence — PASS
All eight factors are grounded in the research trace:
- **Factor 1** (status quo, active as of May 12): Confirmed by KOLD News article (May 12, 2026) and research entry showing Nanos giving official Nancy Guthrie investigation updates. Multiple national outlets (The Hill, Newsweek, IBTimes, Hello Magazine) referenced him as active sheriff in May 2026.
- **Factor 2** (Board voted NOT to remove, May 12-13): Confirmed by multiple web search results — KOLD, AZ Luminaria, KGUN9, Fox News, ktar.com all report the Board declined to remove Nanos. Supervisor Christy's motion failed without a second; Rex Scott moved to take no action; perjury allegations were referred to AG on a 4-0 vote with one abstention.
- **Factor 3** (Recall requires 120 days): Confirmed by the Arizona law research entry citing ARS Title 19, Ballotpedia, and the Arizona Constitution. "Signature gathering period: 120 days" and "Total realistic timeline: ~6-9 months."
- **Factor 4** (AG perjury referral ~10 days ago): Referral was May 12-13, question date is May 22 = ~10 days. Correct per trace.
- **Factor 5** (FBI investigation, no imminent charges): Question background confirms FBI is "separately probing whether Nanos delayed FBI involvement in the Nancy Guthrie missing-person case." No search results indicate imminent charges.
- **Factor 6** (No signs of resignation): Research entry explicitly states: "No reports of resignation, removal, suspension, or any other status change were found in any source." Web searches for resignation returned only calls FOR resignation, not actual resignation.
- **Factor 7** (9 days to resolution): May 22 to May 31 = 9 days. Correct.
- **Factor 8** (Residual risk): Standard tail-risk factor, no specific factual claim to verify.

### Double-counting — MINOR (no impact)
Factors 1 (status quo) and 6 (no signs of resignation) draw on overlapping evidence — both rely on the KOLD May 12 article and research entry showing Nanos actively serving. However, they capture slightly different concepts (current office status vs. behavioral signals about resignation intent). Factors 2 (Board vote failed) and 7 (9 days) are mildly correlated but genuinely distinct (mechanism exhaustion vs. temporal constraint). The agent explicitly acknowledged factor correlation in the trace ("factor sum implies 99.9% but... factors are somewhat redundant") and adjusted accordingly. No material impact on the forecast.

### Wrong-direction factors — PASS
All seven positive-logit factors correctly push toward YES. The single negative-logit Factor 8 (-0.5) correctly captures residual NO risk. All signs are correct.

### Contradictory assessment — PASS
The assessment concludes strongly YES at 96%, consistent with all factors collectively favoring YES.

### Resolution criteria — PASS
Criteria are fully provided and well-engaged. The agent addresses all removal mechanisms mentioned in the criteria (resignation, removal, recall, death, formal suspension). The fine print about formal suspension via court order is implicitly covered through Factor 4 (AG timeline) and Factor 8 (emergency court order as tail risk). The primary source (pimasheriff.org) is identified in the question background. The May 31 MST deadline is correctly noted.

### Probability assessment — PASS
Anchor is ~99%+ for a 9-day window. Agent adjusts to 96%, a ~3pp departure. The adjustment is well-justified by the extraordinary political pressure: FBI probe, AG perjury referral, no-confidence vote (241-65), Board vote that nearly happened, massive media scrutiny. My independent estimate: 95-97%. The Board vote was the critical inflection point and it passed. Recall is procedurally impossible. AG and FBI investigations take months minimum. The only realistic NO path is voluntary resignation, and Nanos shows no signs of it. 96% is squarely appropriate.

### Weak counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is substantive: it constructs a genuine alternative narrative involving negotiated resignation deals, lawyer advice to limit legal exposure, FBI moving quickly, and behind-closed-doors negotiations. It identifies specific "what would change my mind" signals (personal attorney for resignation negotiations, AG fast-tracking, sealed indictment, Nanos going silent, emergency Board session). This is not a token gesture.

### Overconfident self-assessment — PASS
Confidence of 0.82 is reasonable. The evidence is strong and the window is short, but genuine uncertainty exists about behind-closed-doors dynamics.

### Anchor divergence — PASS
The ~3pp departure from a 99%+ anchor is modest and well-supported by the specific circumstances (multiple active pressures on Nanos). No concern.

### Worldview consistency — PASS
The worldview research entries (chris-nanos-pima-county-sheriff and arizona-law-sheriff-removal) are fully consistent with the factors. The Nancy Guthrie forecast (43541, May 19) also references Nanos as actively serving sheriff, consistent with this forecast. No contradictions found.

### Tool audit — PASS
Agent claims "No tool failures" — ground truth shows 0 errors across 7 calls (0.0% error rate). Consistent.

### Pre-publication event — N/A
This is a forward-looking question about whether Nanos remains sheriff through May 31. No pre-publication resolution concern.

## Summary
No errors found. All factual claims are well-grounded in the trace. The probability of 96% is well-calibrated given the evidence — strong status quo, exhausted removal mechanisms, procedurally blocked recall, early-stage investigations, and a very short 9-day window. The minor factor overlap is acknowledged by the agent and appropriately handled.
